title: Fails to create rootless netns
body:
### Issue Description

I'm running podman inside a a Virtualbox VM (Rocky Linux) on my Ubuntu host system.

7 containers are running. From time to time, when I boot up the VM again, the containers fail to start (they are running in rootless mode and started using Quadlet).

The logs indicate that something fails with setting up the netns. The containers go through a loop of restarts by systemd, until eventually another error comes up regarding IP address allocation (looks very similar to https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18615, which is supposedly fixed in 4.8, while I am running 5.2.2).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Unfortunately I don't have any idea how to reproduce this. Podman runs without problems for a while, also VM reboots and power-offs are no issue until all of a sudden things go wrong again. I wish I could provide some precise steps.

### Describe the results you received

Systemd service (generated by Quadlet) fails to start:
```
Jan 22 16:47:03 vbox systemd[694]: Starting traefik.service...
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox podman[1357]: 2025-01-22 16:47:04.145663341 +0000 UTC m=+0.201975522 container create e5d4df364d473fc917ee87137554c78ea69b032b8c3d81cc32c0f028823f966e (image=dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.1, name=traef>
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox podman[1357]: 2025-01-22 16:47:04.09747131 +0000 UTC m=+0.153783507 image pull 075808f3fdf72baa7b647b63631bf5fee7d143164049ebfc40a54d9f238d4b83 dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.1
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox podman[1357]: 2025-01-22 16:47:04.278834145 +0000 UTC m=+0.335146331 container remove e5d4df364d473fc917ee87137554c78ea69b032b8c3d81cc32c0f028823f966e (image=dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.1, name=traef>
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox traefik[1357]: Error: rootless netns: create netns: open /tmp/containers-user-1002/containers/networks/rootless-netns/rootless-netns: file exists
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=126/n/a
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox systemd[694]: Failed to start traefik.service.
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 1.
Jan 22 16:47:04 vbox systemd[694]: Stopped traefik.service.
```

The file indeed exists, as well as the corresponding pid file. No process is running though with this pid:
```
[pulp@vbox rootless-netns]$ ls -la
total 12
drwx------ 3 pulp pulp 106 Dec 16 11:23 .
drwx------ 4 pulp pulp  84 Jan 23 16:50 ..
-rw------- 1 pulp pulp   1 Dec 16 11:24 ref-count
-rw-r--r-- 1 pulp pulp  60 Dec 16 11:23 resolv.conf
-rw------- 1 pulp pulp   0 Dec 16 11:23 rootless-netns
-rw------- 1 pulp pulp   6 Dec 16 11:23 rootless-netns-conn.pid
drwx------ 4 pulp pulp  33 Dec 16 11:23 run
[pulp@vbox rootless-netns]$ cat rootless-netns-conn.pid 
16522
[pulp@vbox rootless-netns]$ ps aux | grep 16522
pulp       36794  0.0  0.0   6408  2176 pts/1    S+   10:43   0:00 grep --color=auto 16522
```

Then a loop of restarts with the same error follows. At some point the IP address pool is exhausted and the errors look like this:
```
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: Starting traefik.service...
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox podman[59059]: 2025-01-23 02:55:11.622213283 +0000 UTC m=+0.041797034 container create 9381bfa69bc1755035aa1942ed4884aeabdd4b70fa3ee48d5958daa50f147096 (image=dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.3, name=trae>
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox podman[59059]: 2025-01-23 02:55:11.670531125 +0000 UTC m=+0.090114884 container remove 9381bfa69bc1755035aa1942ed4884aeabdd4b70fa3ee48d5958daa50f147096 (image=dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.3, name=trae>
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox podman[59059]: 2025-01-23 02:55:11.607151849 +0000 UTC m=+0.026735633 image pull 88eafdd76c933a76798a389d994b4fdd6b5edb89d702aae10c4350ecaa3febb9 dockerhub.internal/traefik:3.3
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox traefik[59059]: Error: IPAM error: failed to find free IP in range: 10.89.0.1 - 10.89.0.254
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Main process exited, code=exited, status=126/n/a
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Failed with result 'exit-code'.
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: Failed to start traefik.service.
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: traefik.service: Scheduled restart job, restart counter is at 37.
Jan 22 16:52:11 vbox systemd[694]: Stopped traefik.service.
```
Which seems similar to what is described in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18615 and was supposedly fixed in 4.8. Yet I see the same error message with 5.2.2.

Once I delete `rootless-netns` and `rootless-netns.pid` (as mentioned in the first error) and restart the service, the container starts without problems.

### Describe the results you expected

- Rootless netns parts are cleaned up properly in case they failed previously
- IP address pool is not exhausted when containers fail to start

### podman info output

```yaml

  arch: amd64
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

No

### Additional information

_No response_

comments.body:
>   runRoot: /tmp/containers-user-1002/containers

Is that on tmpfs? In order for podman to properly function this dir must be removed on each boot (using tmpfs is easiest)

In general if the path is under `/tmp` on a systemd system that is already a sign that you used podman not from a proper login session at one point which we do not recommend, i.e. by using `su` to switch to the podman user.
see https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md#solution-28

If you are fine with remocing everything you can run `podman system reset` and then just run `podman info` again when you are in a proper systemd login session then it should use a new runroot under `/run/user/$UID`

I have no idea if this changes anything regarding the actual bug here or not but if the dir is not removed on a reboot then that certainly causes some weird issues.



Looking at code I think I know how to trigger this in theory. Somehow the actual netns was unmounted but the underlying file was left there. Then the code tries to access the netns which fails but the file and then we try to create a new netns but since the file is still there that errors.
@Luap99 it seems to be a recurring problem when the users do not have a proper user session.  What do you think about https://github.com/containers/storage/pull/2233 ?

Thank you @Luap99 for the hint regarding `runRoot`. Indeed `/tmp` is not a `tmpfs` on our systems. I did run `podman system reset` on my local VM and could confirm that the `runRoot` is now located at `/run/user/$UID`. When restarting the VM and letting systemd start the containers, the `runRoot` remains at this location and everything seems fine.

I still have to figure out where we are doing something wrong with the setup though. We use ansible for configuring the system and creating the Quadlet files for the container user. Also lingering is in use for that user. But obviously somewhere things go wrong.

Is there any way to change the `runRoot` on a system without resetting whole podman? For our production systems I'd like to keep the volumes at least. I could copy them before running the reset. Yet I was wondering where there is another way to achieve this.

> I still have to figure out where we are doing something wrong with the setup though. We use ansible for configuring the system and creating the Quadlet files for the container user. Also lingering is in use for that user. But obviously somewhere things go wrong.

AFAIK ansible does not use one of the "proper" login methods that create the systemd user session. I think it uses `su` (or at least behaves like it when switching to a user). Since podman caches the paths on first use if ansible runs a podman command from the context it might default to the /tmp location.


> Is there any way to change the runRoot on a system without resetting whole podman? For our production systems I'd like to keep the volumes at least. I could copy them before running the reset. Yet I was wondering where there is another way to achieve this.

There is currently no option to do that with podman unfortunately. The main issue is that we store full paths in the db so a external path change could cause very weird inconsistencies. The problem is described in https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/24938


What I don't understand if the paht was removed after boot we should throw an error since https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/3560ccd5dfa4816aae02ec323d7967beae2755ee (v5.1.0) and your podman info lists 5.2.2 so I wonder why this would run at all after boot.

> AFAIK ansible does not use one of the "proper" login methods that create the systemd user session. I think it uses su (or at least behaves like it when switching to a user). 

It does support other methods such as `machinectl` as well, see

```
  become: true
  become_user: pulp
  become_method: machinectl
```

However it throws some other errors now, that I need to look at. I'll come back here once I have found a solution.

> What I don't understand if the paht was removed after boot we should throw an error since https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/3560ccd5dfa4816aae02ec323d7967beae2755ee (v5.1.0) and your podman info lists 5.2.2 so I wonder why this would run at all after boot.

~Maybe I can shed some light on this.~ (never mind, I misunderstood your comment/the code). Anyway, here is what it looks like on a system where `runRoot` is still on `/tmp/`:

```
# podman info | grep -i runRoot
  runRoot: /tmp/containers-user-1002/containers

# find / -name alive
/run/libpod/alive
/run/user/1002/libpod/tmp/alive

# cat /proc/sys/kernel/random/boot_id
987eaf33-0d7c-4009-a31f-74d2ee5523ae

# cat /run/user/1002/libpod/tmp/alive
987eaf33-0d7c-4009-a31f-74d2ee5523ae

# cat /run/libpod/alive
987eaf33-0d7c-4009-a31f-74d2ee5523ae
```

The IDs are all the same, which is not surprising looking at the paths of the files. As the `alive` file is not stored somewhere on `runRoot` but on a `tmpfs` path, it can obviously not contain the state from before the reboot.

I am a bit confused though about https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/libpod/runtime_linux.go#L51. Shouldn't this always fail as the file is gone after a reboot, when it is stored on a `tmpfs` mount?

I have successfully set up a VM from scratch using ansible and making sure that all podman (and systemd) commands are executed using `machinectl` for privilege escalation. This way the `runRoot` path is now set correctly to `/run/user/$UID` from begin with.

In case anyone else is interested how to make this work with ansible:

Add the following to your podman related tasks:

```
  become: true
  become_user: <username>
  become_method: community.general.machinectl
  become_exe: 'sudo machinectl'
```

Furthermore it is important that pipelining is disabled in the SSH connection settings! It is disabled by default, but when you have enabled it for faster ansible execution (like I did), the above settings will fail with non-meaningful ansible errors. Disabling SSH connection pipelining for the respective tasks can be done with

```
  vars:
    ansible_ssh_pipelining: false
```

See https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/latest/collections/ansible/builtin/ssh_connection.html#parameter-pipelining 

With those changes I expect that the netns and IPAM errors which I experienced are gone now. Thank you again @Luap99 for the hint!

I will leave this issue open for the maintainers to decide whether to tackle the possibly non-working reboot detection logic, which should have warned about this misconfiguration of the `runRoot` path, in this issue or a separate one.

Thanks, didn't know ansible can be changed to use machinectl. That is certainly good to know for me.

We can keep the issue open, the actual bug is that we must remove the file ourself in that case (or not make it throw EEXIST).
While in your case the issue was due the tmpdir issue it is possible to break it by killing the podman process in the right moment, i.e. between the file is create and we mount the netns there. Then all following commands will error out in the same way as here. So that needs fixing no matter what IMO
